text.title: Billing Models and Tier Design
text.summary: Billing model selection table, 3-tier design rules, feature distribution pattern, and annual pricing psychology.

list.item: Subscription: ongoing value, sticky usage, requires retention focus (SaaS tools)
One-time: discrete deliverable, no ongoing obligation (courses, templates, guides)
Usage-based: variable consumption, scales with value delivered (API calls, storage, seats)
Freemium: growth-first, network effects, PLG products — requires free tier that creates upgrade pressure
Tiered: different customer sizes, different budgets — most B2B products
Hybrid: subscription base + usage overage — complex value delivery with floor and upside

rule.statement: Design tiers backwards from desired ARPU. The middle tier should have 70-80% of Pro features at 40-50% of Pro price; this is where most users land (decoy effect).
rule.rationale: The decoy effect makes the middle tier appear as the rational choice. Designing backwards from ARPU ensures the middle tier conversion actually achieves revenue targets.

rule.statement: Use a 2-3x price jump between adjacent tiers. Smaller jumps do not create sufficient upgrade motivation; larger jumps create a dead zone where no tier fits mid-market customers.
rule.rationale: Price jump magnitude sets the perceived value gap between tiers. A 2-3x jump is psychologically separable; a 1.2x jump is not.

rule.statement: Do not give everything in the middle tier. Reserve at least one compelling feature for Pro that addresses a real pain of mid-tier users.
rule.rationale: Without a genuine Pro upgrade trigger, the middle tier becomes the permanent stopping point and upgrade conversion collapses.

section.title: Annual pricing

rule.statement: Frame annual pricing as "2 months free" rather than "17% off" or "20% discount". Identical math; "2 months free" has significantly higher take-up in A/B tests.
rule.rationale: Concrete gift framing (months free) is more actionable than percentage savings. The equivalent math does not change retention or cash flow but does change conversion rate.

rule.statement: Implement explicit grandfather policy for price increases: "we honor your price for 12 months". Silent grandfathering forever erodes margin; silent removal destroys trust.
rule.rationale: Documented, time-limited grandfathering is predictable and builds trust with early adopters without permanently subsidizing them at new-customer CAC.

section.reference: Tier structure section of the pricing report template
